PURPOSE

The Investigation Training Précis are maintained by the Investigative Standards Unit. The précis are written to offer guidance and assistance to all members of the Western Australia Police Force (WA Police Force) in relation to police investigations.

The précis highlight the theory and methodology underpinning ‘Criminal Investigations’ and are not intended to be referenced as Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).

Should information regarding SOPs be sought then readers should refer to the Frontline Applications ‘Investigative Standards’ link accessed via the WA Police Force standard operating environment computer desk top.

The précis are an ‘all of agency’ resource.

INTRODUCTION

Every offence in the Criminal Code commences with ‘any person’; it is the first element of every offence and every element of an offence must be proved beyond reasonable doubt.

It is this identification element to which this precis relates, the ‘who dunnit’ component of a criminal investigation. Officers may identify a ‘suspect’ during an investigation but the evidence required to substantiate any involvement is complicated.

Identification processes are the methods officers will apply in order to identify the person involved in an offence. Predominantly the involvement will be that of the offender but occasions will occur when the processes are used to identify witnesses.

The identification of an accused is a critical element that must be proved by the prosecution.

Evidence must be produced that clearly identifies the accused as the person who committed the offence.

There are numerous ways in which police will present identification evidence to a court; most of these methods are well grounded in law. The purpose of this subject will be to highlight some of the processes available, give instruction on how to conduct them and importantly, draw the reader’s attention to some of the pitfalls associated with the varying methods.

From the outset it should be stressed that officers should always attempt to seek as many forms of identification as possible, as each method has the potential to be challenged in court.

The identification of suspects and accused persons may be accomplished in a number of ways:

• visual identification

• fingerprint evidence

• DNA evidence

• handwriting evidence

• voice identification

• propensity evidence

• admissions by a suspect or accused person

• a combination of the above.

Officers must be mindful that the above list is not exhaustive but acts as a guide to the most common forms of identification. The intuitive nature of Police Officers will identify the most appropriate method of identifying the offender.

This subject will deal solely with visual identification evidence, as other identification methods are the responsibility of experts in a specialist field.

No matter how the identification is adduced, officers must always keep in mind the words of the Honourable Chief Justice GIBBS:

“No matter what the form of identification used, it is the duty of police officers investigating crime to take every precaution reasonably available to guard against the miscarriage of justice that can occur1.”

The précis are an ‘all of agency’ resource. It is this identification element to which this precis relates, the ‘who dunnit’ component of a criminal investigation. There are numerous ways in which police will present identification evidence to a court; most of these methods are well grounded in law. Officers must be mindful that the above list is not exhaustive but acts as a guide to the most common forms of identification. The intuitive nature of Police Officers will identify the most appropriate method of identifying the offender. This subject will deal solely with visual identification evidence, as other identification methods are the responsibility of experts in a specialist field. No matter how the identification is adduced, officers must always keep in mind the words of the Honourable Chief Justice GIBBS: “No matter what the form of identification used, it is the duty of police officers investigating crime to take every precaution reasonably available to guard against the miscarriage of justice that can occur1.”

sections.h:
Identification processes is a component of the Police Manual2 listed under ‘Identifying Suspects’ and officers should always reference the subject to ensure they are complying with contemporary standards.

There are two types of evidence that may be adduced regarding the accused’s criminal responsibility for an offence:

• identification evidence; and

• recognition evidence.

Recognition evidence deals with a witness that for some reason knows the accused i.e.

blood relationship, past friendship, or general acquaintance. In these circumstances, the identification can quite simply be attested to in a statement which should reflect the nature of the relationship.

It is the other form that this subject will primarily deal with. In a situation where a witness has seen, but does not know the identity of the suspect, particular care must be taken by officers when obtaining visual identification evidence.

Once a suspect has been nominated and visual identification becomes critical there is an onus on police to use a more formal method to establish visual identification.

These are the acceptable methods of visual identification:

• digi-boards and photo-boards;

• identification parades (formal police line-up); and

1 Alexander V R (1980-81) 145 CLR 395

2 Police Manual, IS – Identification of Suspects

• group identification (e.g. using a busy street, shopping centre or railway station).

sections.h:
Digi-boards and photo-boards are tools which officers can use to visually identify suspects or accused persons. Each is a series of mixed images in which the suspect or accused person may or may not be placed in random order. The images other than that of the accused are selected or digitally enhanced to represent persons of similar age, appearance, race, etc. of that of the suspect or accused person. This is to ensure fairness.

Numbers, rather than names, are used to identify the photographs or images because the true identity of each person is never revealed to the witness.

Once compiled, the digi-board is shown to the witness who then attempts to identify the person they recognise as being the suspect or accused person.

Unlike a conventional photograph, digi-board images can be altered by the Forensic Imaging Unit (FIU) to ensure the board will provide the fairest possible visual identification method.

For example, background colours and lighting conditions can be matched and distinguishing features of suspects can be duplicated upon other images. The board is protected from alteration so integrity is maintained.

Digi-boards are the preferred type of identification board and should always be used in preference to a conventional photo-board.

The following rules were shaped by the Criminal Practices and Procedures Review Committee in 20023 and are to be followed by any police officer attempting a visual identification procedure.

Unless these rules are followed, evidence of visual identification may not be admissible unless the Court is satisfied that there are exceptional circumstances and applying ‘Christie Discretion’4 (to exclude admissible prosecution evidence which is ‘more prejudicial than probative”).

The rules are as follows:

1. The photograph identification board must contain at least 12 computer generated images

(digi-boards) or 12 photographs (photo-boards) of persons of similar appearance.

2. Where reasonably practicable the identification procedure is to be audio-visually

recorded so that the procedure will be visible if required by the courts.

3. In all cases proper records are to be kept of the identification procedure by the police

officers concerned unless that is not reasonably practical.

4. The records of identification procedures made by police officers are to be available for

perusal, at a reasonable time, by counsel for an accused person on request.

3 Supreme Court of Western Australia, Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions, Aboriginal Legal Service, Legal Aid, Criminal Law Society, WA Police Force and Crown Law Department, Criminal Practices and Procedures Review Committee, 2002.

4 R v Christie [1914] AC 545

5. The Formatted Interview Form (Witness Conversation) must be completed and retained,

along with the audio/audio-visual record of the procedure. (NOTE: A new format and form was introduced in January 2023 named; Image Identification Interview (Adult Witness/Child Witness) Form, which will be sent with the digi-board, or it can be found here on the Investigative Standards Blue Book intranet page).

6. The identification procedure must involve only one witness at a time.

7. After a witness has taken part in the identification procedure, the witness must be asked

not to speak about the procedure, as far as reasonably practical, to any other identifying witness until all identification procedures in the investigation have been conducted.

8. A police officer must not prevent a person being present to support a prospective witness

during an identification procedure unless;

a. The second person is a witness involved in the procedure; or b. The officer suspects that the second person will influence the prospective witness's decision or disrupt the procedure.

9. If a police officer prevents someone being present during the procedure to support the

prospective witness the police officer must;

a. Give the prospective witness the reason for preventing the other person being present; and

b. Advise the prospective witness that he or she may arrange for someone else to be present for support; and

c. If asked, allow someone else to be present

10. Where practicable, the identification interview should be conducted by a police officer

independent to the inquiry. Officers are urged to use an independent person to ensure there can be no allegations of impropriety.

Request Procedure

Where digi-boards or photo-boards are deemed necessary to an investigation they may only be compiled and distributed by the Forensic Imaging Unit.

The following procedures are to be adopted by officers seeking to use digi-boards or photo- boards.

1. Ensure that the digi-board and photo-board is necessary for the inquiry remembering

that multiple orders of an extremely speculative nature will not be proceeded with unless there are extenuating circumstances;

2. Ensure that a photograph of the suspect is in existence;

3. Email to Forensic Imaging Unit SMAIL providing the following details;

a. Full name, date of birth and docket number of a suspect; and b. IMS number to which the offence relates.

4. If more than one image of the suspect exists, nominate which image is to be used.

All digi-boards, unless specifically requested or prevented through circumstance, will be compiled in digital format and forwarded to the investigating officer via email and in .PDF (portable document format).

Whether the digi-board is conveyed electronically or physically, it must be accompanied by a formatted interview form in .doc (word document) format.

Officers must also be mindful to only use a digi-board from Forensic Imaging Unit and to use a different digi-board for each witness.

Things not to do

Do not contaminate your witness prior to an identification procedure. You would be well served to use an independent person for the entire process, from the attendance of the witness at the unit or station to the completion of the identification process. This will negate any claims that the witness was influenced in some way to make a specific selection. Police officers need to ensure that the identification is conducted with fairness and without prejudice to the suspect or accused person and does not prejudice a defence.5 Do not deviate from the formatted Image Identification Interview form, it is important that the format is followed precisely. This format has been designed to minimise any claims of influence.

Do not make changes to a digi-board in anyway, this includes changing the images and marking the digi-board in some form.

Do not use the same digi-board for different witnesses as this negates any claims there was collusion between witnesses regarding which number they selected.

Do not provide feedback to the witness regarding their choice. This will impact on their memory no matter whether they have selected the suspect or not.

The conduct of digi-board or photo-board identification is reasonably simple and sound in law, the processes are well documented and if adhered to will give the identification the best chance of admissibility.

Investigators can now consider digi-boards as a first option and assuming the guidelines for their use are strictly adhered to, their admission in court will be likely.

It is important however, not to discount identification parades and group identification as alternative or complimentary forms of visual identification. Although identification parades and group identifications are often impractical or impossible to conduct for a variety of reasons, it is essential that investigators consider them as an option.

sections.h:
There may be instances where the use of an identification parade is more appropriate than a digi-board or photo-board. Such instances may be when:

• The accused person or suspect requests an identification parade; or

• No other forms of identification are available (e.g. no access to photograph or digital

image of the accused or suspect).

Investigators should be aware that the use of a formal identification parade is very rare, but knowledge of the process is important.

If the decision is made to conduct a parade, the following points needs to be considered by the investigating officer.

5 Police Manual IS-01.00

Rights of the Accused or Suspect

An identification parade cannot be conducted without the consent of the accused or suspected person.

When requesting consent, police officers must record the request and the person's response on audio or audio-visual recording. If those facilities are not available, then a written record must be made of the questions and answers.

The suspect or the accused person must be told that they may have a legal representative present during the conduct of the identification parade. Every effort should be made to accommodate that request as far as is practicable.

If the suspect or accused person is a juvenile (under 18 years), he or she must be accompanied by a legal representative, parent or guardian; but that person is not allowed to speak during the parade; influence the decision of the witness or otherwise interfere with the conduct of the parade. This person shall be so instructed before the identification parade is conducted.

In the event of the suspect or accused person not being able to speak English or being hearing impaired, officers must ensure that an independent, professionally trained and if possible, accredited interpreter is present7.

If the suspect or accused person is not fluent in English, then the guidelines by the Supreme Court of Northern Territory in R v. Anunga and other (1976) should be followed8.

Setting up a Parade

The identification parade must be conducted in a location, which provides favourable conditions and adequate security. For example, there must be adequate lighting to ensure a fair identification. Additionally, a person that has been arrested may pose a risk of escape.

This will need to be considered and managed.

An identification parade must consist of at least nine persons including the suspect or accused person.

As far as possible, people participating in an identification parade must be of similar appearance to the suspect or the accused person and resemble the suspect or accused person in age, height and general appearance.

Police officers must not participate in an identification parade, unless the suspect or accused person is a police officer.

As far as practicable, all unauthorised persons should be excluded from the place where the identification parade is being held.

7 Police Manual IT-01.00 Interpreters and Translators – Use of (Professionals) 8 Police Manual IT-01.00 as above n7.

Conduct of the Parade

The courts, legal representatives and other external bodies may question the process by which an identification parade is made to determine its fairness and admissibility. The following guidelines will assist officers in the conduct of the identification parade:

• To demonstrate fairness and impartiality, an officer who is not attached to the

investigative team should conduct an identification parade.

• Obtain the consent of the accused or suspected person and record the request and

responses:

o Audio-visually, or if not available;

o on audio, or if audio is not available;

o by written means.

• Obtain the consent of each witness proposed to participate in the line-up and record

his or her response.

• Consideration must be given to the safety of the witness. The accused person should

not, at any stage, have the opportunity to either identify the witness or pose a physical threat to the witness.

• Where practicable, record the witness’s viewing of the line-up using audio-

visual/audio recording devices and if not practicable, the parade line-up must be photographed. See “Recording the Identification Parade Process” below.

• There must only be one suspect or accused person in an identification parade at any

one time.

• The suspect or the accused person shall pick his or her position in the identification

parade.

• If there is more than one witness to the identification parade, the witnesses must view

the line-up individually and not be able to communicate with each other, or be in a position where they might overhear other witnesses.

• If there is more than one witness to the identification parade, then the suspect or the

accused person may change his position after each witness has viewed the identification parade.

• Every precaution must be taken to ensure no prompting; suggestion or hint is given

to the witness that any particular member of the identification parade is the suspect.

• The attending police or investigating officers will not enter into discussion or make

any suggestions to the witness regarding the description of the suspect or accused person.

Whilst it is entirely proper during the investigative phase of any inquiry to use identikits, photo-boards or other photographic material to establish the identity of a suspect; it is unfair and improper to show a witness, immediately prior to holding of an identification parade, an identikit; a photograph or photo-board of the suspect or accused. To do so may refresh or taint the witness's actual memory and may render evidence of the identification inadmissible.

Recording the Identification Parade Process

In order to demonstrate fairness in the identification process, the parade line-up should be audio-visually recorded. If this is not practicable, then the parade line-up must be photographed. (R v Penny)9.

9 R v Penny (1997) IND 314 of 1996

Every effort must be made to record everything spoken during the parade. This should include the precise words of the witness either identifying or not identifying the suspect or the accused person. The recording of the exact words spoken will enable the strength of the identification (or failure to identify) to be demonstrated. If video or audio facilities are not available, then a written record must be kept of the comments made by the witness.

When the identification parade has been completed and the witness has departed, the officer conducting the identification parade shall ask the suspect or the accused person whether he or she wishes to make any comments on the conduct of the parade. That question, and any comments made, should be audio/audio-visually recorded if the facilities are available, or otherwise in writing.

When not to use an Identification Parade

An identification parade should not be used when:

• The suspect or accused person does not wish to participate in the process.

• The witness or witnesses do not wish to participate in the process.

• There are no suitable facilities for conducting the parade, i.e. No suitable viewing

room or similar facility.

• There are not a suitable number of persons of similar description available

(Remembering that these persons must also participate of their own free will).

• In the case of accused persons, the security of the accused cannot be maintained or

is at risk.

• The safety or wellbeing of the witnesses or other line-up participants is at risk.

• The preparation of an identification parade would take an unreasonable length of

time.

• The financial resources needed to conduct such an identification are unavailable or

unreasonable.

• In the case of juveniles (under 18 years of age) no suitable parent, guardian or legal

representative is available to be present during the process.

Where a suspect or accused person requests an identification parade but the decision is made not to conduct such a parade, it is incumbent upon the investigating officer to record all reasons why such a decision was made.

These decisions must be recorded in a running sheet or decisions log and the reasons made available to the courts or a legal representative upon request.

Another form of identification is group identification, again the use of these is very rare but knowledge of the process is important.

An identification parade must consist of at least nine persons including the suspect or accused person. As far as possible, people participating in an identification parade must be of similar appearance to the suspect or the accused person and resemble the suspect or accused person in age, height and general appearance. Police officers must not participate in an identification parade, unless the suspect or accused person is a police officer. As far as practicable, all unauthorised persons should be excluded from the place where the identification parade is being held. 7 Police Manual IT-01.00 Interpreters and Translators – Use of (Professionals) 8 Police Manual IT-01.00 as above n7. Conduct of the Parade The courts, legal representatives and other external bodies may question the process by which an identification parade is made to determine its fairness and admissibility. The following guidelines will assist officers in the conduct of the identification parade: • To demonstrate fairness and impartiality, an officer who is not attached to the investigative team should conduct an identification parade. • Obtain the consent of the accused or suspected person and record the request and responses: o Audio-visually, or if not available; o on audio, or if audio is not available; o by written means. • Obtain the consent of each witness proposed to participate in the line-up and record his or her response. • Consideration must be given to the safety of the witness. The accused person should not, at any stage, have the opportunity to either identify the witness or pose a physical threat to the witness. • Where practicable, record the witness’s viewing of the line-up using audio- visual/audio recording devices and if not practicable, the parade line-up must be photographed. See “Recording the Identification Parade Process” below. • There must only be one suspect or accused person in an identification parade at any one time. • The suspect or the accused person shall pick his or her position in the identification parade. • If there is more than one witness to the identification parade, the witnesses must view the line-up individually and not be able to communicate with each other, or be in a position where they might overhear other witnesses. • If there is more than one witness to the identification parade, then the suspect or the accused person may change his position after each witness has viewed the identification parade. • Every precaution must be taken to ensure no prompting; suggestion or hint is given to the witness that any particular member of the identification parade is the suspect. • The attending police or investigating officers will not enter into discussion or make any suggestions to the witness regarding the description of the suspect or accused person. Whilst it is entirely proper during the investigative phase of any inquiry to use identikits, photo-boards or other photographic material to establish the identity of a suspect; it is unfair and improper to show a witness, immediately prior to holding of an identification parade, an identikit; a photograph or photo-board of the suspect or accused. To do so may refresh or taint the witness's actual memory and may render evidence of the identification inadmissible. Recording the Identification Parade Process In order to demonstrate fairness in the identification process, the parade line-up should be audio-visually recorded. If this is not practicable, then the parade line-up must be photographed. (R v Penny)9. 9 R v Penny (1997) IND 314 of 1996 Every effort must be made to record everything spoken during the parade. This should include the precise words of the witness either identifying or not identifying the suspect or the accused person. The recording of the exact words spoken will enable the strength of the identification (or failure to identify) to be demonstrated. If video or audio facilities are not available, then a written record must be kept of the comments made by the witness. When the identification parade has been completed and the witness has departed, the officer conducting the identification parade shall ask the suspect or the accused person whether he or she wishes to make any comments on the conduct of the parade. That question, and any comments made, should be audio/audio-visually recorded if the facilities are available, or otherwise in writing. When not to use an Identification Parade An identification parade should not be used when: • The suspect or accused person does not wish to participate in the process. • The witness or witnesses do not wish to participate in the process. • There are no suitable facilities for conducting the parade, i.e. No suitable viewing room or similar facility. • There are not a suitable number of persons of similar description available (Remembering that these persons must also participate of their own free will). • In the case of accused persons, the security of the accused cannot be maintained or is at risk. • The safety or wellbeing of the witnesses or other line-up participants is at risk. • The preparation of an identification parade would take an unreasonable length of time. • The financial resources needed to conduct such an identification are unavailable or unreasonable. • In the case of juveniles (under 18 years of age) no suitable parent, guardian or legal representative is available to be present during the process. Where a suspect or accused person requests an identification parade but the decision is made not to conduct such a parade, it is incumbent upon the investigating officer to record all reasons why such a decision was made. These decisions must be recorded in a running sheet or decisions log and the reasons made available to the courts or a legal representative upon request. Another form of identification is group identification, again the use of these is very rare but knowledge of the process is important.

sections.h:
Group identification is a process by which a witness is asked to identify a suspect or accused person from a group or crowd of people in a busy street, shopping centre, hotel, pedestrian mall, railway station etc.

10 Police Manual IS-01.00 Group Identification and Procedures.

The process of group identification should only occur in circumstances when the suspect or accused person refuses to participate in a formal identification parade, when it is impossible or impracticable to hold one or if the suspect or accused person specifically requests it.

Once again however, consent must be obtained before this alternative form of identification is used. In this regard the Courts have said: "Where an accused person has specifically refused to engage in an identification parade to be conducted by police, the police may properly adopt any alternative approach to his identification, and if there is some element of suggested unfairness in the form of identification adopted which does not infringe his right to refuse to be obliged to enter an identification parade, he brings this on himself by obliging the police to adopt other means to secure identification"11.

The decision to use a group identification process in preference to a formal identification parade is one which investigating officers must be able to justify to the satisfaction of a court, should the identification process be contested.

Group identification is still acceptable as long as the police officer can show fairness and propriety. It should follow the same principles as those for holding an identification parade.

To demonstrate fairness, the group identification scene must be recorded on video or photographed. This will help show that the identification process has been done impartially and without prejudice to the suspect or accused person.

The importance of taking audio-visual recordings or photographs of the process was highlighted in R v Penny12 where a group identification was conducted on a railway platform.

Wallwork J said:

Additionally, there were no photographs taken of the persons on the relevant (railway) platform and no video of what happened. Due to the lack of photographic evidence as to the number of persons on the platform and their description, which is available to the jury, is not as reliable as it could have been. This is despite the well-intentioned efforts of the police officers that should have been aware of the correct procedures to be followed13.

In any event, at the least, in my view the proceedings should have been videotaped or photographed. I rule that the evidence as to the identification procedure at the Perth Railway Station should not be admitted at the trial14.

During a group identification and where possible, the audio-visual recording device should be positioned behind the witness and recording the process as the witness would be viewing it, so the court may view (as best as possible) the process in much the same way.

The conversation (if any) made by the witness during the process must be recorded by the audio-visual recording device or if not available, separate audio or by written record. This will also assist the court in determining the value of the identification.

11 R v Smith (1984) 12 A. Crim. R. 439

12 R v Penny, as above n9.

13 R v Penny, as above n9.

14 R v Penny, as above n9.

When not to use Group Identification

The group identification method of visual identification should not be used when:

• The suspect or accused person does not wish to participate in the process.

• The witness or witnesses do not wish to participate in the process.

• There are no suitable facilities for conducting the parade or group identification, i.e.

No suitable public areas for group identification.

• In the case of accused persons, the security of the prisoner cannot be maintained or

is at risk.

• The safety or wellbeing of the witnesses or members of the public is at risk.

• The preparation of the group identification would take an unreasonable length of time.

• The financial resources needed to conduct such an identification are unavailable or

unreasonable.

• In the case of juveniles (under 18 years of age) no suitable parent, guardian or legal

representative is available to be present during the process.

Where a suspect or accused person requests group identification but the decision is made not to conduct such identification, it is incumbent upon the investigating officer to record all reasons why such a decision was made. These decisions must be recorded in a running sheet or decisions log and the reasons made available to the courts or a legal representative upon request.

Thus far, three visual identification methods have been explored. The decision of which to use will always be dictated by the circumstances of the inquiry, however the following will offer some guidance in this area.

Where a suspect or accused person requests group identification but the decision is made not to conduct such identification, it is incumbent upon the investigating officer to record all reasons why such a decision was made. These decisions must be recorded in a running sheet or decisions log and the reasons made available to the courts or a legal representative upon request. Thus far, three visual identification methods have been explored. The decision of which to use will always be dictated by the circumstances of the inquiry, however the following will offer some guidance in this area.

sections.h:
The courts originally held that the best method of obtaining identification of a suspect or accused person by visual means is by way of a properly conducted identification parade.

It was also said that, if properly carried out, an identification parade is the most reliable means of identification15. However, in 2002 the Criminal Practices and Procedures Review Committee determined that the continued endorsement of identification parades (formal police line-ups) and group identifications was not only outdated, but also largely unachievable in their application16.

The committee acknowledged that digitally enhanced photographs (digi-boards) are a preferable alternative technology, which can achieve more cost efficient and effective outcomes to not only the WA Police Force and the judiciary, but also the community as a whole.

Investigators can now consider digi-boards as a first option and assuming the guidelines for their use are strictly adhered to, their admission in court will be likely.

15 Cross On Evidence 5th Aust Edition at Page 74

16 Supreme Court of Western Australia, Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions, Aboriginal Legal Service, Legal Aid, Criminal Law Society, WA Police Force and Crown Law Department, Criminal Practices and Procedures Review Committee, 2002.

With all of the process and procedure associated with formal identification, it is still possible that errors can occur. The following will indicate some of the reasons for GIBBS CJ17 comments earlier in this text.
sections.b: The courts originally held that the best method of obtaining identification of a suspect or accused person by visual means is by way of a properly conducted identification parade. It was also said that, if properly carried out, an identification parade is the most reliable means of identification15. However, in 2002 the Criminal Practices and Procedures Review Committee determined that the continued endorsement of identification parades (formal police line-ups) and group identifications was not only outdated, but also largely unachievable in their application16. The committee acknowledged that digitally enhanced photographs (digi-boards) are a preferable alternative technology, which can achieve more cost efficient and effective outcomes to not only the WA Police Force and the judiciary, but also the community as a whole. Investigators can now consider digi-boards as a first option and assuming the guidelines for their use are strictly adhered to, their admission in court will be likely. 15 Cross On Evidence 5th Aust Edition at Page 74 16 Supreme Court of Western Australia, Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions, Aboriginal Legal Service, Legal Aid, Criminal Law Society, WA Police Force and Crown Law Department, Criminal Practices and Procedures Review Committee, 2002. With all of the process and procedure associated with formal identification, it is still possible that errors can occur. The following will indicate some of the reasons for GIBBS CJ17 comments earlier in this text.

sections.h:
The criminal justice system relies heavily on eyewitnesses to determine the facts surrounding criminal events18. Eyewitnesses may identify offenders, recall conversations, or remember other details.

An eyewitness who has no motive to lie is a powerful form of evidence for jurors, especially if the eyewitness appears to be highly confident about his or her recollection19.

In the absence of definitive proof to the contrary, judges and juries generally accept the eyewitness’s account20.

However, the faith the legal system places in eyewitnesses has been shaken recently by the advent of forensic DNA testing. Given the right set of circumstances, forensic DNA testing can prove that a person who was convicted of a crime is, in fact, innocent21.

In the United States alone an analysis of DNA exoneration cases since 1992 reveal that mistaken eyewitness identification was involved in the vast majority of these convictions, accounting for more convictions of innocent people than all other factors combined22.

But even decades before the advent of forensic DNA testing, psychologists were questioning the validity of eyewitness reports. But it was not until the mid-to late 1970s that psychologists began to conduct programmatic experiments aimed at understanding the extent of error and the variables that govern error when eyewitnesses give accounts of crimes they have witnessed23.

Many of the experiments conducted in the late 1970s and throughout the 1980s resulted in articles by psychologists that contained strong warnings to the legal system that eyewitness evidence was being overvalued by the justice system in the sense that its impact on triers of fact (juries) exceeded its probative (legal-proof) value24.

Another message of the research was that the validity of eyewitness reports depends a great deal on the procedures that are used to obtain those reports and that the legal system was not using the best procedures25.

17 Alexander V R (1980-81) 145 CLR 395

18 Gary L Wells, Amina Memon and Steven D. Penrod, Eyewitness Testimony, Psychological Science in the Public Interest, Association for Psychological Science, 2006, Volume 7 Number 2.

19 Wells et al, as above n 18

20 Wells et al, as above n 18

21 Wells et al, as above n 18

22 Wells et al, as above n 18

23 Wells et al, as above n 18

24 Wells et al, as above n 18

25 Wells et al, as above n 18

Case Study #1

Kirk Bloodsworth had never been in trouble with the law, and yet he was convicted in March 1985 for the 1984 sexual assault and slaying of a 9-year-old girl in Maryland (State of Maryland v Kirk N. Bloodsworth 1984)26.

Five eyewitnesses identified Bloodsworth at trial and later that month, a judge sentenced him to death. He spent 2 years on death row before he received a new trial based on evidence that the prosecution had withheld information about other suspects.

The new trial resulted in Bloodsworth receiving a life sentence. Bloodsworth maintained a claim of innocence from the outset, but it was not until 1993 that he was released from prison on the basis of DNA testing that proved he was not the source of semen found in the little girl's underwear.

Despite his release from prison, some people, including one of the original prosecutors, continued to believe that Bloodsworth might still be the murderer. The eyewitness evidence just seemed too strong.

Theories evolved that maybe Bloodsworth really was the murderer and the tiny speck of semen came from someone other than the murderer - perhaps from someone who had access to the little girl's dresser drawer.

Bloodsworth went on with his life, confident in his own innocence but having to live with the occasional doubt raised by those who somehow remained un-persuaded of his innocence.

Then, in September 2003, DNA testing got a hit on the actual murderer, Kimberly Shay Ruffner. Nineteen years after Kirk Bloodsworth was sentenced to death, the proof was finally there: He had had nothing to do with the sexual assault and slaying of the young girl. The case of Kirk Bloodsworth illustrates several problems with eyewitness evidence.

First, it illustrates the fallacy of assuming that multiple witness agreement is necessarily strong evidence of accuracy. Many factors can lead to multiple witness agreement, such as interaction among the witnesses in which they share information. In general, factors that lead one eyewitness to make a particular error may lead others to make the same error.

Second, the Bloodsworth case illustrates the profound level of proof required for exonerating evidence to trump eyewitness identification evidence. Even when the semen was proved not to match Bloodsworth's DNA, many people were unwilling to believe he was innocent. It was necessary to prove that someone else had committed the murder.

Third, the Bloodsworth case illustrates that mistaken identification is a dual problem: Not only might an innocent person be convicted but the guilty party remains free to re-offend.

Case Study #2

On 24 July 1989, Harold Blackburn, a former Superintendent in the NSW Police Service was arrested and later charged with 13 counts of sexual assault and other offences. His 26 State of Maryland v Kirk N. Bloodsworth 1984

spectacular arrest included notification to the media and the walking of Blackburn through the media contingent.

The investigation team led by Detective Chief Inspector Thornwaite, Detective Senior Sergeant Carl Spain and Detective Sergeant Minkley constructed a case against Blackburn based largely on Thornwaite’s suspicion that Blackburn knew the area in which the rapes occurred and that he bore a resemblance to a sketch of the rapist.

The rapes for which Blackburn was arrested and charged occurred in two groups. The first series took place between 1969 and 1970 and the second series took place nearly twenty years later.

During the course of the investigation into the 1985-1988 rapes, Thornwaite, Spain and Minkley ignored:

• Fingerprint evidence that was favourable for Blackburn;

• Reports from three rape victims that Blackburn was not the rapist; and

• Substantial forensic evidence which excluded Blackburn from being the attacker.

The one positive identification of Blackburn as the attacker was made by one of the victims from the first series of assaults. After prompting by Minkley some 20 years after the attack, the victim identified Blackburn using a current photograph. Later, one of the male victims of a 1980 attack offered a 75% identification of Blackburn, even though his original statement said the attacker had worn a mask completely covering his face.

The morning after the arrest of Blackburn, Sergeant Minkley was involved in a car accident and suffered severe head injuries. Inspector Clive Small was brought into the investigation to fill in for Minkley and became responsible for preparing the brief of evidence. Small found the evidence to be in such disarray that he re-interviewed a number of the witnesses and made some of his own inquiries. He quickly found that the preponderance of the evidence supported Blackburn.

The Blackburn case had many investigative flaws such as groupthink and confirmation bias, but from a visual identification perspective the following was identified:

• Multiple witnesses were shown the same photo-board which may have been

marked with pen by other witnesses.

• Subsequent witnesses picked the marked photograph.

• Investigators also wrote their interpretation of the witnesses’ response rather than

the witnesses’ actual words.

This formed the basis of the review of photo-boards such as:

• Separate photo-boards for each witness;

• Video corroboration; and

• Witness reply recorded verbatim. i.e. “That is the man”; “Similar to him”; “Could

be”.

Additionally, it was identified that the psychological processes leading to eyewitness error represent a confluence of memory and social-influence variables that interact in complex ways27.

27 Wells et al, as above n 18

sections.h:
The eyewitness identification of the accused will be subject to many factors. These factors will have differing effects on different witnesses.

Stress, emotional arousal and or trauma as a result of the incident will be dependent on the role of the witness during the situation. What else was happening at the time will affect the amount of time the witness would have been concentrating on the accused. A substantial time difference between the incident and the request for recollection will affect the witness’s memory.

Tunnel vision during event, e.g. If weapons are involved the witness’s eyes may have been fixated on the weapon and they may have very little recollection of the surroundings and other events thus leading to a limited ability to identify an offender.

The legal history of witness identification and recent scientific research shows honest witnesses can make errors in identifying offenders.

Case Study #3

Twice in July 1984, an assailant broke into apartments in North Carolina, severed phone wires, sexually assaulted a woman, searched through her belongings, and took money and other items. On August 1, 1984, Ronald Cotton28 was arrested for these crimes.

In January 1985, Cotton was convicted by a jury of one count of rape and one count of burglary. On appeal, the North Carolina Supreme Court overturned the 1985 conviction because the second victim had picked another man out of the line-up and the trial court had not allowed this evidence to be heard by the jury.

In November 1987, Cotton was retried, this time for both rapes because the second victim had decided that Cotton was in fact her assailant. Prior to the commencement of the second trial, a prison inmate, told another inmate that he had committed the crimes for which Cotton had been convicted. A Superior Court Judge refused to allow this information into evidence and Cotton was convicted of both rapes.

The prosecutor's evidence at trial included a photograph identification made by one of the victims, a police line-up identification made by one of the other victims, a flashlight found in Cotton's home that resembled the one used by the assailant and a tennis shoe belonging to Cotton, the rubber of which matched rubber found at the scene.

In 1994, the chief appellate defender requested a motion for DNA testing that was granted in October 1994. In the spring of 1995, the Burlington Police Department turned over all evidence that contained the assailant's semen for DNA testing.

The samples from one victim were too deteriorated to be conclusive, but the samples from the other victim's vaginal swab and underwear were subjected to PCR based DNA testing and showed no match to Cotton. At the defence’s request, the results were sent to the State Bureau of Investigation's DNA database, containing the DNA patterns of 28 State of North Carolina V Cotton 351 S.E 2d 277 (1987) convicted violent felons in North Carolina prisons. The state's database showed a match with the convict who had earlier confessed to the crime.

On June 30, 1995, Cotton was officially cleared of all charges and released from prison.

In July 1995, the governor of North Carolina officially pardoned Cotton, making him eligible for $5,000 compensation from the state. Cotton had served 10.5 years of his sentence.

Arising from this case is the incredible story of Jennifer Thompson, the victim who had identified Cotton. An aspiring college student at the time of the crime, she made it her purpose to study the assailant's face on the photo so that he would be brought to justice.

She identified the wrong man. Today, Ms. Thompson accepts that Cotton is not the assailant and accepts that the DNA evidence is factual, but she still sees Cottons face as that of the offender

This case study highlights the inaccuracy of witness identifications.

The preceding case studies highlight the vulnerability of eyewitness identification; this vulnerability has also been recognised by the courts and incorporated into case law.
sections.b: The eyewitness identification of the accused will be subject to many factors. These factors will have differing effects on different witnesses. Stress, emotional arousal and or trauma as a result of the incident will be dependent on the role of the witness during the situation. What else was happening at the time will affect the amount of time the witness would have been concentrating on the accused. A substantial time difference between the incident and the request for recollection will affect the witness’s memory. Tunnel vision during event, e.g. If weapons are involved the witness’s eyes may have been fixated on the weapon and they may have very little recollection of the surroundings and other events thus leading to a limited ability to identify an offender. The legal history of witness identification and recent scientific research shows honest witnesses can make errors in identifying offenders. Case Study #3 Twice in July 1984, an assailant broke into apartments in North Carolina, severed phone wires, sexually assaulted a woman, searched through her belongings, and took money and other items. On August 1, 1984, Ronald Cotton28 was arrested for these crimes. In January 1985, Cotton was convicted by a jury of one count of rape and one count of burglary. On appeal, the North Carolina Supreme Court overturned the 1985 conviction because the second victim had picked another man out of the line-up and the trial court had not allowed this evidence to be heard by the jury. In November 1987, Cotton was retried, this time for both rapes because the second victim had decided that Cotton was in fact her assailant. Prior to the commencement of the second trial, a prison inmate, told another inmate that he had committed the crimes for which Cotton had been convicted. A Superior Court Judge refused to allow this information into evidence and Cotton was convicted of both rapes. The prosecutor's evidence at trial included a photograph identification made by one of the victims, a police line-up identification made by one of the other victims, a flashlight found in Cotton's home that resembled the one used by the assailant and a tennis shoe belonging to Cotton, the rubber of which matched rubber found at the scene. In 1994, the chief appellate defender requested a motion for DNA testing that was granted in October 1994. In the spring of 1995, the Burlington Police Department turned over all evidence that contained the assailant's semen for DNA testing. The samples from one victim were too deteriorated to be conclusive, but the samples from the other victim's vaginal swab and underwear were subjected to PCR based DNA testing and showed no match to Cotton. At the defence’s request, the results were sent to the State Bureau of Investigation's DNA database, containing the DNA patterns of 28 State of North Carolina V Cotton 351 S.E 2d 277 (1987) convicted violent felons in North Carolina prisons. The state's database showed a match with the convict who had earlier confessed to the crime. On June 30, 1995, Cotton was officially cleared of all charges and released from prison. In July 1995, the governor of North Carolina officially pardoned Cotton, making him eligible for $5,000 compensation from the state. Cotton had served 10.5 years of his sentence. Arising from this case is the incredible story of Jennifer Thompson, the victim who had identified Cotton. An aspiring college student at the time of the crime, she made it her purpose to study the assailant's face on the photo so that he would be brought to justice. She identified the wrong man. Today, Ms. Thompson accepts that Cotton is not the assailant and accepts that the DNA evidence is factual, but she still sees Cottons face as that of the offender This case study highlights the inaccuracy of witness identifications. The preceding case studies highlight the vulnerability of eyewitness identification; this vulnerability has also been recognised by the courts and incorporated into case law.

sections.h:
Courts will tend to treat with caution uncorroborated identification evidence. This does not mean uncorroborated identification is insufficient to gain a conviction, however a warning29 will be given to the jury.

A single image of a suspect should never be shown to a witness for identification purposes, likewise, a witness should never be shown the suspect in person, unless in the form of an identification parade30.

The court will always treat with caution an identification during which there is some indication to the witness from police they suspect the person31.
sections.b: Courts will tend to treat with caution uncorroborated identification evidence. This does not mean uncorroborated identification is insufficient to gain a conviction, however a warning29 will be given to the jury. A single image of a suspect should never be shown to a witness for identification purposes, likewise, a witness should never be shown the suspect in person, unless in the form of an identification parade30. The court will always treat with caution an identification during which there is some indication to the witness from police they suspect the person31.

sections.h:
The Harrison warning is the equivalent of the McKinney warning regarding police confessional evidence.

Where the identification is an issue and the Crown case depends wholly or substantially upon identification by strangers, the following principles should be observed:

1. Trial judge should warn jury of the special need for caution before convicting the

accused.

29 Harrison v R (1987) WASC 308 (7 October 1987)

30 Craig v The King (1933) 49 CLR 429, 439

31 Craig, as above n23

2. He should instruct the jury as to the reason for the need for such a warning and

make some reference to the possibility that a mistaken witness could be a convincing one.

3. He should direct the jury to examine closely the circumstances in which ID came

about, including whether the witnesses had seen the accused before & discrepancies in appearance between the description given & accused’s actual appearance.

4. He should remind the jury of any specific weaknesses in the identification evidence.

5. He should take care in directing the jury as to the support for an identification which

might be derived from the rejection of an alibi.

6. He should tell the jury that the absence of the accused from the witness box could

not provide evidence of anything, although they might take into account in assessing the quality of the identification evidence that it was not contradicted by evidence from the accused himself.

There are many nuances when dealing with the identification of a suspect or suspects.

Investigators should as a matter of prudence, remain conversant with the law as it relates to identification evidence.

SUMMARY

As stated earlier, the legal history of witness identification and recent scientific research shows honest witnesses can make errors in identifying an accused.

As demonstrated in the preceding pages there are many different case law decisions that apply to identification evidence; additionally, there can be weaknesses in witness testimony that may affect identification. The courts will always be cautious of identification evidence that has not been obtained correctly; as such this evidence may be excluded where the probative value of the evidence is outweighed by the danger of prejudice to the accused.

Officers must always conduct the identification in accordance with contemporary WA Police Force procedures and be conversant with leading case law to ensure that this valuable form of evidence is not excluded during trial.

Always remember that both inculpatory and exculpatory identification evidence must be disclosed to the defence.

BIBLIOGRAPHY

Alexander V R (1980-81) 145 CLR 395.

Cross On Evidence 5th Aust Edition at Page 74.

Craig v The King (1933) 49 CLR 429, 439.

Gary L Wells, Amina Memon and Steven D. Penrod, Eyewitness Testimony, Psychological Science in the Public Interest, Association for Psychological Science, 2006, Volume 7 Number 2.

Harrison v R (1987) WASC 308 (7 October 1987).

Police Manual: IS - Identification of Suspects

Police Manual: IT - Interpreters and Translators

R v Penny (1997) IND 314 of 1996.

R v Christie [1914] AC 545.

R v Smith (1984) 12 A. Crim. R. 439.

State of Maryland v Kirk N. Bloodsworth 1984

State of North Carolina V Cotton 351 S.E 2d 277 (1987)

Supreme Court of Western Australia, Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions, Aboriginal Legal Service, Legal Aid, Criminal Law Society, WA Police Force and Crown Law Department, Criminal Practices and Procedures Review Committee, 2002.

sections.h:
IS-01.02 Digi-Board Identification Procedures

Request a digi-board from Forensic Imaging Unit. This will contain 12 computer generated images of persons of similar appearance.

To comply with Police Manual IS-01.02, follow the below identification procedures:

 A Police Officer independent to the investigation should conduct the Digi-board Identification process.

 The identification procedure must involve only one witness at a time.

 A separate digi-board is to be used for each witness.

 The digi-board is to be placed into a plain folder with no other markings visible (ie a plain manila folder).

 The Digi-board Identification process should be audio-visually recorded.

 The relevant Image Identification Interview (Adult/Child) Form, sent with the digi-board, or found on the Investigative Standards Blue Book intranet page, must be completed and retained along with the audio-visual record of the procedure.

 If an identification is made, it is advisable the witness initials within the numbered box as well as completing and signing the bottom of the form.

 After a prospective witness has taken part in the identification procedure, the witness must be asked not to speak about the procedure to any other identifying witness, until all identification procedures in the investigation have been conducted.

 An officer must not disclose to a witness if they were successful or not in identifying a suspect.

 A person may be present to support a witness during the identification procedure, unless they are also a witness to the incident under investigation. They should be instructed to not disrupt or influence the witness’ decision making.

 An officer may prevent a support person being present, but must outline to the witness why their support person is unable to be present and allow another person to be present if requested.

 In all cases proper records are to be kept of the identification procedure by the police officers concerned unless that is not reasonably practical.

 The records of identification procedures made by police officers are to be available for perusal, at a reasonable time, by counsel for an accused person on request.

Unless these rules are followed, evidence of attempted digi-board identification will not be admissible in evidence in a trial unless the court is satisfied there are exceptional circumstances that, in the interests of justice, justify the admission of the evidence.
